title: Dairy goat daily health checks
subtitle: What to look at every day to catch problems in a small dairy goat herd before they become serious.
excerpt: A dairy goat tells you a great deal in the first 30 seconds of the morning milking. Her appetite, her posture, the look of her coat, and the feel and output of her udder are the daily indicators that something is right or wrong. Building the observation habit makes early detection automatic rather than something that happens once a week.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Animal Welfare Codes for goats, gov.uk. Smith and Sherman, Goat Medicine (2009), Wiley-Blackwell: daily health monitoring chapter. SAC Consulting Scotland: dairy goat husbandry.

glossaryTerms.definition: The process by which a goat regurgitates partially digested food (cud) from the rumen and chews it again. A goat at rest should be visibly chewing cud. Absence of rumination for several hours is one of the first signs of rumen dysfunction or systemic illness.

A colour chart system for assessing anaemia in small ruminants by comparing the colour of the inner lower eyelid (conjunctiva) to a five-point scale. Score 1 is red-pink (no anaemia); score 5 is white (severe anaemia from barber's pole worm). Developed in South Africa but used as a complementary tool in UK flocks.

A metabolic condition caused by insufficient energy intake relative to demand, most common in does in the last weeks of pregnancy or early lactation. The doe stops eating, appears dull, and may show neurological signs. The urine and breath smell sweet or pear-drop-like. Requires immediate intervention.

content.text: The morning routine for a dairy goat herd includes the health check. The check takes very little additional time because most observations happen during normal milking and feeding activity. The discipline is making them systematic rather than incidental.
On arrival at the pen

Note which goats rise to the gate and which do not. A goat that stays lying when the others move at feeding time is a first-alert sign. Assess posture of any goat lying down: natural rest is a goat on her chest or side with legs loosely folded. A goat lying awkwardly, repeatedly getting up and down, or pressing her flank against a wall suggests abdominal pain.
Checking rumination
At rest after feeding, goats should be visibly
ruminating

. The jaw moves in a steady sideways chew, often with the eyes half-closed. A goat not ruminating at rest is worth watching. If she has not ruminated for a full morning and is off her feed, check her temperature (normal is 38.5 to 39.5 C rectally) and contact your vet.
At the milking stand
The milking routine provides systematic close contact with every doe in the herd. Use it. As you wipe the udder before milking:
Run both hands over the udder halves. Note any heat, hardness, or asymmetry.
Check the skin between the teats and on the teat skin for sores, cracks, or blisters.
Foremilk the first few squirts and check for clots or discolouration.
Note milk yield: if a doe who normally gives 2 litres produces 1.2 litres with no other explanation, something is changing.
Eyes and mucous membranes
Once a week, check the inner lower eyelid colour of each doe using the
FAMACHA score

. Pull the lower eyelid gently down and compare the pink of the conjunctiva to the reference card. A score of 3 or above warrants a worm egg count. FAMACHA is not reliable as a standalone worming decision tool, but it catches the most anaemic animals quickly as a daily observation.
Late pregnancy and early lactation: extra vigilance
The two weeks before kidding and the two weeks after freshening are the highest-risk period for
ketosis

. A doe going off feed in the last days of pregnancy, or not eating well in the week after kidding, needs propylene glycol supplementation immediately and a vet call if she does not improve within 12 hours. Ketosis progresses quickly.
